title: A Modern, Thriving Society (Part 2)
overview: The United Kingdom today is a diverse, modern, and creative country. Its people come from many backgrounds but share the same values of fairness, democracy, and respect for the law. The UK's economy has evolved from manufacturing to services, technology, and innovation. Education, healthcare, and equality are key parts of modern life.

study_sections.heading: Population and Language
study_sections.content: The UK's population is around 67 million people. The official language is English, but Welsh, Gaelic, and Irish are also spoken. The UK's currency is the pound sterling (£). London is the capital and one of the world's leading financial centres. Other major cities include Birmingham, Manchester, Glasgow, Cardiff, and Belfast.

study_sections.heading: Economy and Innovation
study_sections.content: The economy is based mainly on services, technology, and creative industries. The UK is known for its education, health system (NHS), and innovation. Britain remains a global leader in science, technology, and the arts.

study_sections.heading: Rights and Values
study_sections.content: The Equality Act (2010) protects people from discrimination. The Human Rights Act (1998) guarantees freedom and fairness. British society values respect, inclusion, and diversity. The Union Jack represents the unity of the four nations.

study_sections.heading: National Identity
study_sections.content: The UK celebrates both national and cultural events, reflecting its mixed heritage. Modern Britain thrives on diversity, fairness, and shared opportunity. Citizens enjoy freedom of belief, expression, and opportunity.

key_facts: Service industries such as finance, education, health, retail and hospitality make up most of the UK economy.
The UK is a world leader in science and innovation, home to universities like Oxford, Cambridge and Imperial College London.
Creative industries—film, television, design, music and video games—contribute billions of pounds each year.
Major cultural festivals include the BBC Proms, Edinburgh Festival Fringe and Glastonbury Festival.
The BBC, founded in 1922, provides public service broadcasting on television, radio and online.
National museums and galleries offer free admission, making art and heritage accessible to everyone.
Sport is part of daily life, with football, rugby, cricket, athletics and tennis enjoyed nationwide.
People increasingly choose leisure activities such as walking, cycling, gym membership and visiting National Parks.
The UK has committed in law to reach net-zero greenhouse gas emissions by 2050, encouraging green industries.
Digital technology—broadband, smartphones and online services—shapes work, learning and communication.
memory_hook: Think of modern Britain as a lively marketplace: science stalls, creative studios, sports pitches and green-tech kiosks all buzzing together, powered by public services like the BBC.
flashcards: What is the UK's official language?
